summary: This experience report describes how Xapo Bank transformed its architecture practice from a centralized bottleneck to a decentralized, advice-based model. Originally a Bitcoin custodian that pivoted to become a licensed bank, Xapo faced challenges with technical debt, globally distributed teams across 40+ countries, and slow decision-making through a traditional architecture function. By implementing the Architecture Advisory Forum (AAF) based on Andrew Harmel-Law's conversational approach, combined with Domain-Driven Design and Team Topologies reorganization, Xapo empowered teams to make architectural decisions independently while seeking advice from stakeholders. The result was dramatically faster decision-making—from weeks or months down to days—with better documentation and collective ownership of architecture.
key_points: Centralized architecture teams create bottlenecks, remove responsibility from implementing teams, and suffer from long feedback loops disconnected from real implementation challenges
The Architecture Advisory Forum (AAF) uses an advice process where anyone can make architectural decisions after seeking input, without requiring consensus or approval
ADRs were moved from Confluence to Jira to track decision throughput metrics, adding an 'adopted' status to measure time from draft to production deployment
Domain-Driven Design language and bounded contexts emerged naturally through ADR discussions, accelerating understanding and revealing coupling issues between teams
The approach requires ongoing care—consensus-seeking habits persist, and regular curation of the ADR backlog with dedicated ownership proved essential for sustained success
money_quotes: Architecture, to the teams which actually build them, is literally 'someone else's problem'

It's somewhat fitting that a company founded around the principles of decentralised access to finance should choose to manage architecture in this way, fully decentralised and without the need for a central approving authority
Designs and decisions that used to take weeks (or months!) now happen in days, are well documented, understood by all and form part of the collective intelligence of our technical community
The comfort of consensus is a hard thing to let go of
Team autonomy without guidance and direction results in chaos. Constraints like the Advice Process (including responsibility) helped set Xapo free
People saw first hand designs, including organisational designs, don't need to be right the first time
Don't get too hung up on getting it all perfect, get it good enough and make progress
key_insight: Decentralizing architecture decisions to empowered teams, guided by an advice process rather than approval gates, dramatically accelerates delivery while improving collective ownership and understanding.
